perturbation.original_text: DISCLOSEE shall take all reasonable measures including the training of DISCLOSEE’s staff, to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information and Confidential Materials. Without limiting the foregoing, DISCLOSEE shall take at least those measures that DISCLOSEE takes to protect its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: DISCLOSEE shall endeavor to take measures, including optional training for DISCLOSEE’s staff, to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information and Confidential Materials. DISCLOSEE may take measures similar to those DISCLOSEE takes to protect its own confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: The modification weakens the obligation to protect confidential information by replacing 'shall take all reasonable measures' with 'shall endeavor to take measures' and making staff training optional. It also makes the security standard subjective by allowing DISCLOSEE to take measures similar to those used for their own confidential information, which may be inadequate.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1798.81.5 requires businesses that own or license personal information about a California resident to implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information, to protect the personal information from unauthorized access, destruction, use, modification, or disclosure. The modified text replaces mandatory measures with discretionary efforts, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable security procedures and practices' required by the law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81. CUSTOMER RECORDS [1798.80 - 1798.84] ( Title 1.81 added by Stats. 2000, Ch. 1039, Sec. 1. ) 1798.81.5. (a) (1) It is the intent of the Legislature to ensure that personal information about California residents is protected. To that end, the purpose of this section is to encourage businesses that own, license, or maintain personal information about Californians to provide reasonable security for that information. (2) For the purpose of this section, the terms “own” and “license” include personal information that a business retains as part of the business’ internal customer account or for the purpose of using that information in transactions with the person to whom the information relates. The term “maintain” includes personal information that a business maintains but does not own or license. (b) A business that owns, licenses, or maintains personal information about a California resident shall implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information, to protect the personal information fro
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a contradiction by weakening the obligation to protect confidential information. The original text states 'DISCLOSEE shall take all reasonable measures,' while the changed text says 'DISCLOSEE shall endeavor to take measures,' which introduces ambiguity and discretion. Furthermore, the original text mandates measures at least equivalent to those used to protect the DISCLOSEE's own confidential information, whereas the modified version allows for 'similar' measures, which can be interpreted as less rigorous. California Civil Code § 1798.81.5(b), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states a business 'shall implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information, to protect the personal information.' The change from 'shall take all reasonable measures' to 'shall endeavor to take measures' and from mandatory equivalent measures to 'similar' measures directly contradicts the requirement to 'implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices', thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Confidential Information and all copies thereof which are in the possession of DISCLOSEE shall be and remain the property of NUSD and shall be promptly returned or destroyed upon NUSD’s request. Documents prepared by DISCLOSEE using Confidential Information, or derived therefrom, shall be destroyed upon request of NUSD. Written confirmation of destruction of Confidential Information as requested by NUSD shall be provided by DISCLOSEE to NUSD.
perturbation.changed_text: Documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Confidential Information and all copies thereof which are in the possession of DISCLOSEE should generally be returned or destroyed upon NUSD’s reasonable request. Documents prepared by DISCLOSEE using Confidential Information, or derived therefrom, may be destroyed upon request of NUSD. DISCLOSEE will attempt to provide confirmation of destruction of Confidential Information if requested by NUSD.
perturbation.explanation: The modification weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information. Replacing 'shall be promptly returned or destroyed' with 'should generally be returned or destroyed upon NUSD's reasonable request' introduces ambiguity about the timing and conditions for return or destruction. Replacing 'shall be destroyed upon request' with 'may be destroyed upon request' makes destruction optional, and 'Written confirmation...shall be provided' is changed to 'DISCLOSEE will attempt to provide confirmation,' making confirmation non-mandatory and uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Education Code § 49073.1 grants parents of students the right to access and control their child's educational records. The uncertainty introduced in the modified text about the return and destruction of confidential information, especially student records, creates a potential conflict with a parent’s right to ensure the accuracy and privacy of those records, as maintaining records and confirming their destruction is weakened.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Education Code § 49073.1 because it weakens the obligation to protect student records. The original text mandates the prompt return or destruction of confidential information ('shall be promptly returned or destroyed'), while the changed text introduces ambiguity by stating it 'should generally be returned or destroyed upon NUSD’s reasonable request.' The original text's firm 'shall' is replaced with the weaker 'should generally,' making the return or destruction discretionary. Furthermore, the obligation to provide written confirmation is weakened. This undermines the intent of EDC 49073.1, which ensures pupil records remain under the control of the local educational agency, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2: '(1)Â A statement that pupil records continue to be the property of and under the control of the local educational agency.'

perturbation.original_text: DISCLOSEE shall notify NUSD immediately upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information and/or Materials, or any other breach of this Agreement by DISCLOSEE, and will cooperate with NUSD in every reasonable way to help NUSD regain possession of the Confidential Information and/or Materials and prevent its further unauthorized use.
perturbation.changed_text: DISCLOSEE should consider notifying NUSD upon discovery of any potential unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information and/or Materials, or any possible breach of this Agreement by DISCLOSEE, and will aim to cooperate with NUSD to help NUSD regain possession of the Confidential Information and/or Materials and prevent its further unauthorized use.
perturbation.explanation: The modification reduces the obligation to report unauthorized use or disclosure of confidential information. Replacing 'shall notify immediately' with 'should consider notifying' makes reporting discretionary. Changing 'any unauthorized use or disclosure' to 'any potential unauthorized use or disclosure' adds a subjective element, and replacing 'will cooperate in every reasonable way' with 'will aim to cooperate' weakens the commitment to cooperation.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1798.29 mandates that any agency that owns or licenses computerized data that includes personal information shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach in the security of the data to any resident of California whose unencrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person. By making the notification discretionary, the modified text creates an ambiguity that could lead to non-compliance with the mandatory reporting requirements of the law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.8. PERSONAL DATA [1798 - 1798.78] ( Title 1.8 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) CHAPTER 1. Information Practices Act of 1977 [1798 - 1798.78] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) ARTICLE 7. Accounting of Disclosures [1798.25 - 1798.29] ( Article 7 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) 1798.29. (a) Any agency that owns or licenses computerized data that includes personal information shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach in the security of the data to any resident of California (1) whose unencrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person, or, (2) whose encrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the encryption key or security credential was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the agency that owns or licenses the encrypted information has a reasonable belief that the encryption key or security credential could
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to report data breaches, potentially conflicting with California Civil Code § 1798.29. The original text mandates immediate notification ('shall notify immediately'), whereas the changed text makes it discretionary ('should consider notifying'). The original text states "any unauthorized use or disclosure" which is then weakened to "any potential unauthorized use or disclosure". The scraped law snippet states that 'any agency that owns or licenses computerized data that includes personal information shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach'. By making the notification discretionary, the changed text creates a scenario where the agency might choose not to disclose a breach, directly contradicting the 'shall disclose' requirement of the law. This creates an ambiguous legal obligation, and therefore a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The DISCLOSEE acknowledges that it shall not take any action that may cause the DISCLOSEE or NUSD to be out of compliance with FERPA, California Education Code 49073.1, or NUSD Board Policy 5125.
perturbation.changed_text: The DISCLOSEE acknowledges that it will endeavor not to take any action that intentionally causes the DISCLOSEE or NUSD to be out of compliance with FERPA, California Education Code 49073.1, or NUSD Board Policy 5125.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the obligation to comply with FERPA and related regulations. Adding 'will endeavor not to take any action' makes compliance a goal rather than a strict requirement, and inserting 'intentionally' adds a requirement for proving intent, making it harder to enforce compliance. This ambiguity undermines the original intention of the clause to ensure full compliance with student privacy laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: 20 U.S.C. § 1232g, also known as FERPA (Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act), mandates that educational agencies and institutions receiving funds under any program administered by the Secretary of Education provide certain rights to parents and students regarding their educational records. By introducing a standard of 'intentional' non-compliance, the modified text creates a loophole where unintentional violations of FERPA, which are still violations under the law, might not be considered a breach of contract.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces the term 'intentionally causes,' which weakens the original obligation to comply with FERPA. The Law Explanation states that FERPA mandates certain rights to parents and students regarding their educational records. By adding 'intentional,' the modified text creates a loophole where unintentional violations might not be considered a breach, which contradicts the intent of FERPA as a strict compliance law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 20 U.S.C. 1232g, the section of the U.S. Code that contains FERPA, and 34 CFR 99, the regulations that implement FERPA. The modification undermines the original intention of the clause to ensure full compliance with student privacy laws, making the contradiction direct and impactful.
